---
title: | 
  Scientific Approach to Understanding Policies
subtitle: "Understanding Policies (10700193-90)"
author: "Yue Hu"
institute: "Tsinghua University" 
bibliography: t_publicPolicy.bib

execute: 
  echo: false
editor_options: 
  chunk_output_type: console
editor: 
  render-on-save: true

format: 
  revealjs:
    number-sections: false
    css: https://www.drhuyue.site/slides_gh/css/style_basic.css
    theme: ../../../css/goldenBlack.scss
    # logo: https://gitlab.com/sammo3182/backup/raw/85b3c1ad4b459d7a9f901f124b936428eda5fcaf/logo_zzxx.png?inline=true
    slide-number: true
    filters: [appExclusion.lua] # not count appendices into page number
    incremental: true
    preview-links: true # open an iframe for a link
    link-external-newwindow: true
    self-contained: false
    chalkboard: true # allwoing chalk board B, notes canvas C
    # callout-icon: false
    show-slide-number: all # `speaker` only print in pdf, `all` shows all the time
    title-slide-attributes:
      data-background-image: https://gitlab.com/sammo3182/backup/raw/85b3c1ad4b459d7a9f901f124b936428eda5fcaf/logo_THPS.png?inline=true
      data-background-size: 250px   
      data-background-position: top 10% right 5%
revealjs-plugins:
  - spotlight
lightbox: 
  match: auto
  effect: fade
spotlight:
  size: 50
  presentingCursor: default
  toggleSpotlightOnMouseDown: false
  spotlightOnKeyPressAndHold: 73 # keycode for "i"
---

## Overview

Goal: Understand the *scientific* approach to analyzing policies

::: {style="text-align:center; margin-top: 2em"}

+ [Dissect]{.red}: What does being "scientific" mean
+ [Process]{.red}: How to conduct a scientific inquiry
+ [Apply]{.red}: When (*not why*) understanding policies scientifically

:::

# Dissect Science

## Paradigms For Understanding Policies

> Paradigm: a bag of theories, research methods, postulations, and standards for what constitutes contributions to a field.

+ Positivist: 
    + Evidence-based
    + Problem-solving through systematic queries
+ Post-positivist
    + *Culture*-based (norms, morality, values)
    + Normative, ethical process

:::{.notes}

Dominant, 实证主义

Re-imported from the French word positivisme(/positee vish men/), derived from positif in its sense of 'imposed on the mind by experience'. 

:::

## {.center background="#43464B"}

::: {.r-fit-text}

Being positivist = being scientific?

:::

## A Story

A TCM^[Traditional Chinese Medicine, TCM] 🧑‍⚕️told a [story](http://szyyj.gd.gov.cn/zyyfw/ysbj/content/post_2541293.html) about drinking water:^[《为什么外国人喝凉水没事》之“每天早晨喝一杯凉水，是一个错误的养生做法”]


> 每天早晨喝一杯凉水，是一个错误的养生做法……[某大历害老医师]是偶然发现这个问题的，当时他在给一位女士调理身体，本来调理得很好……突然有一天，再诊脉的时候，发现她的舌质又淡白了，舌体胖大，病情反复，忙问这是怎么回事儿？她回答，半个月前，看到电视里面有养生专家让晨起喝凉水，于是坚持了这些天，已经腹泻了很多次了。这么样，损失了阳气，导致水湿重。有位中医也被这一说法骗了……最后竟然收集了几百例的病例，都是晨起喝凉水，导致的各种复杂古怪的病症的。

## Question {.center background="#43464B"}

::: {.r-fit-text}

- Will the seniors in your family be convinced by this piece?
- Is it a scientific inquiry?

:::

## Let's Decompose It

:::{.fragment}

*Problem*

"给一位女士调理身体，本来调理得很好……突然有一天，再诊脉的时候，发现她的[舌质又淡白了，舌体胖大，病情反复]{.blue}。"

:::

:::{.fragment}

*Theory*

"[损失了阳气]{.red}，导致水湿重。"

:::

:::{.fragment}

*Evidence*

"晨起喝凉水，于是坚持了这些天，已经[腹泻]{.blue}了很多次了。"

:::

:::{.fragment}

*Conclusion*

"每天早晨喝一杯凉水，是一个[错误]{.red}的养生做法"

:::


## Why Convincing

It's a human inquiry: a question/puzzle -> point + evidence

::: {.callout-note .fragment}

## Example

Q: Why do you Sichuanese/Jiangxiness/Huanese like to eat spicy food?

A: Our place is humid. Spices can "祛湿."

![](https://drhuyue.site:10002/sammo3182/figure/sci_hotpot.jpg){fig-align="center" height=400}

::: {.notes}

后面有解释

:::

:::


## Another Example

![“你递啊，你伸啊！”](https://drhuyue.site:10002/sammo3182/figure/sci_driverCasher.gif){fig-align="center" height=500}


The video shows that the toll collector and the driver were in a standoff for 1 minute.

## Question {.center background="#43464B"}

::: {.r-fit-text}

Why aren't above inquires not [scientific]{.red} inquiries?

:::

::: {.fragment}

$$
\text{Human inquiry} = Point + Evidence.
$$

$$
\text{Scientific inquiry} = \color{red}{Procedure} (Point + Evidence).
$$

:::

# Process Science

## Why not stay with human inquiries

::: {.r-stack}

![](https://drhuyue.site:10002/sammo3182/figure/sci_driverCasher.jpeg){fig-align="center" height=600}

::: {.r-hstack .fragment}

![湃客(2020)](https://drhuyue.site:10002/sammo3182/figure/sci_spicy.jpg){fig-align="center" height=600}

![王静爱、左伟(2010)](https://drhuyue.site:10002/sammo3182/figure/sci_huminity.png){fig-align="center" height=600}

:::

:::

::: {.notes}

- 湃客. 2020. 《如果吃辣有段位，你的家乡排第几？》. 澎湃号. https://www.thepaper.cn/newsDetail_forward_8315589 (2025年2月23日).
- 王静爱和左伟. 2010. 中国地理图集. 中国地图出版社. https://book.douban.com/subject/4733762/ (2025年2月23日).

https://www.bilibili.com/video/BV17RAgeoEvs/?spm_id_from=333.1387.favlist.content.click&vd_source=f38aeefd0d38cecba9017eeee43e71c8

:::

## Risks of human inquiry

:::: {.columns}

::: {.column .nonincremental width="50%"}

- Incomplete information
- Illogical reasoning  
- Selection observation  
- Inaccurate observation  
- Overgeneralization  
- ......

{{< video https://drhuyue.site:10002/sammo3182/video/sci_shangChunShan_dissection.mp4 title=dissection height=300 loading="eager" allowfullscreen>}}

:::

::: {.column .fragment width="50%"}


{{< video https://drhuyue.site:10002/sammo3182/video/sci_shangChunShan_twist.mp4 title=dissection height=300 loading="eager" allowfullscreen>}}


![](https://drhuyue.site:10002/sammo3182/figure/sci_shangChunShan_downstream.png){.fragment fig-align="center" height=300}



:::


::::



## Human &rarr; Scientific Inquiries

<center>
{{< video https://drhuyue.site:10002/sammo3182/video/sci_Charle's_Angle_ScientistScene.mp4 title="A scientific Inquiry always starts with a guess" loading="eager" height=300 allowfullscreen>}}

[**Guess/Hypothesis**]{.fragment}
</center>

:::: {.columns}

::: {.column .fragment width="50%"}

**Data**

- Be [Valid]{.red}
    - Being representative
- Be [Sufficient]{.red}
    - Being diverse

:::

::: {.column .fragment width="50%"}

**Analysis**

- Be [Proper & consistent]{.red}

- Point     
  &rarr; [Data + Method &rarr; Analysis]{.green}     
  &rArr; Evidence/conclusions

:::

::::

:::{.notes}

- Guess: Charle's Angle, 2019, Columbia Pictures, Elizabeth Banks' film
- Data: Solving the incomplete, selective, or inaccurate observation
- Analysis: Solving the overgeneralization and illogical reasoning

:::

## Scientific Procedure

:::: {.columns}

::: {.column width="40%"}

**Three (or Four) Steps**^[No need to complete in one piece.]

1. Lay out (assumptions & hypotheses)
1. Description
1. Explanation
1. (Prediction)  

:::

::: {.column .fragment width="60%"}

**Two Approaches**

Inductive vis-a-vis Deductive

![](https://drhuyue.site:10002/sammo3182/figure/sci_wheelScience.png){fig-align="center" height=400}

:::

::::

## Group discussion {.center background="#43464B"}

::: {.nonincremental style="text-align:center"}

Scientific inquiry:

- Being valid
- Being sufficient
- Being proper & consistent

:::

[Why can the scientific procedure reduce human-inquiry risks?]{.fragment}

::: {.notes}

Scientific inquiry addresses each risk of human inquiry through three requirements:

1. **Being valid** (representative data) → counters *selective observation* and *inaccurate observation*: by requiring data to be representative of the population rather than cherry-picked, it prevents researchers from drawing conclusions from a biased or unrepresentative sample.

2. **Being sufficient** (diverse data) → counters *incomplete information*: by requiring diverse data points, the scientific procedure ensures coverage across different cases and conditions, preventing conclusions drawn from too narrow a base.

3. **Being proper & consistent** (rigorous analysis) → counters *illogical reasoning* and *overgeneralization*: by requiring that methods be appropriate to the research question and applied uniformly, the procedure enforces logical discipline—conclusions must follow from evidence through explicit, replicable steps rather than intuition or informal leaps.

Together, these requirements transform subjective human impressions into intersubjectively verifiable knowledge: the hypothesis-data-analysis chain forces transparency at every step, allowing others to check and challenge the reasoning.

:::

# Apply Science

## What can science do

- Give an answer? [No.^[A true story in the [public policy area](#sec-app-story). ]]{.fragment}
- Win an argument? [Not exactly, but]
    - Provide [acceptable]{.red} evidence
    - Win an argument *among scientists*

::: {.fragment}

<center>
{{< video https://drhuyue.site:10002/sammo3182/video/sci_youngSheldon.mp4 title="Nonscientific debates between scientists" loading="eager" height=350 allowfullscreen>}}
</center>

:::

## Social science

:::{.nonincremental style="text-align:center"}

1. Can human behavior being [scientifically]{.red} researched?
1. Can social scientific results scientifically [reliable]{.red}?
1. Is social science [different]{.red} from natural science?

:::

<center>
{{< video https://drhuyue.site:10002/sammo3182/video/sci_sheldon_socialScience.mp4 title="Sheldon on Social Science" height=400 loading="eager" allowfullscreen>}}
</center>



## When do policymakers need science

Depends on *what kind of conclusion* s/he wants to make:

:::{style="text-align:center; margin-top: 0.5em"}

Credible conclusions vs. Strong conclusions

:::

::: {.callout-important .fragment}

## The Law of Decreasing Credibility

Strength of assumptions [&uarr;]{.blue} &rArr; credibility [&darr;]{.red} 

:::

::: {.fragment}

<center>
{{< video https://drhuyue.site:10002/sammo3182/video/sci_dontlookup.mp4 title="don't look up" height=400 loading="eager" allowfullscreen>}}
</center>

:::


:::{.notes}

- 只要有疫苗了，我们就安全了 vs. 疫苗是战胜新冠病毒之制胜关键

:::


## Power and science

:::: {.columns}

::: {.column width="50%"}

> Ranges are for cattle. Give me a number.

![Lyndon Johnson, the 36th POTUS](https://drhuyue.site:10002/sammo3182/figure/sci_Johnson.jpg){fig-align="center" height=400}

:::

::: {.column .fragment width="50%"}

![Jerry Hausman, MIT economist, Durbin-Wu-Hausman test](https://drhuyue.site:10002/sammo3182/figure/sci_hausman.jpg){fig-align="center" height=350}

> You can't give the client a bound. The client needs a point.

:::

::::

::: {.notes}

Johnson use the anecdote to reply an economist's description of the uncertainty of his forecast

:::

##

::: {.r-fit-text}

Both being [considered]{.red} and being [solid]{.red} can be important

:::

## Question {.center background="#43464B"}

::: {.r-fit-text}

*What's your choice?*

:::

## Take-Home Points

+ [Dissect]{.red} Science: What does being "scientific" mean
    - $\text{Scientific inquiry} = \color{Procedure}{red}(Point + Evidence).$
+ [Process]{.red} Science: How to conduct a scientific inquiry
    - Why human inquiries can be risky?
    - Steps of processing science
+ [Apply]{.red} Science: When (*not why*) understanding policies scientifically
    - Social science
    - Policymakers' view

:::{.notes}

Four steps:

1. Lay out (assumptions & hypotheses)
1. Description
1. Explanation
1. (Prediction)   

Two approaches: deductive and inductive

:::


## Challenge is on the way

::: {style="text-align:center"}

Who are the [lucky dogs]{.red} today??

:::

- Your missions
    - Hansen(2019) 
- Presenter: 
    + Content
    + Connection
    + Inspiring
    + & A question: Examining if the challengers **do read** the article
- Challengers: 
    - A question: Testing whether the presenters understand the article **thoroughly**

# Appendix: Public policy in the real world {.appendix}

## A story about problems brought by science {#sec-app-story}

U.S. Congress, 2010: Congress debated the biggest healthcare reform in decades

- ~50 million Americans had no health insurance
- Democrats proposed the [*Affordable Care Act* (ACA)]{.red}, popularly known as **"Obamacare"**
- Central political battleground: Would it [cost]{.red} the government money or [save]{.red} it?

:::{.notes}

The Patient Protection and Affordable Care Act was signed by President Obama on March 23, 2010, followed by the Health Care and Education Reconciliation Act days later. Together they expanded insurance coverage, introduced insurance market regulations, and created subsidies for low-income Americans. The cost question was pivotal: a bill projected to blow up the deficit would face far greater political resistance.

:::


## Who does the math?

Before Congress votes on major legislation, it must be officially "scored" — i.e., its fiscal impact estimated

:::: {.columns}

::: {.column width="50%"}

**CBO** (Congressional Budget Office)

- Non-partisan agency of Congress
- Produces official cost estimates for all major bills
- Both parties are supposed to trust its numbers

:::

::: {.column width="50%"}

**JCT** (Joint Committee on Taxation)

- Non-partisan; handles revenue/tax side of scoring
- Works alongside CBO on healthcare legislation

:::

::::

:::{.notes}

The CBO was created in 1974 precisely to give Congress an independent, non-partisan source of economic data — a counterweight to the executive branch's Office of Management and Budget (OMB), which serves the President. Its director at the time was Douglas Elmendorf, an economist who later became dean of Harvard Kennedy School.

:::


## Two experts, two conclusions

:::: {.columns}

::: {.column width="50%"}

> Enacting both pieces of legislation...would produce a net reduction of changes in federal deficits of [$138 billion]{.red} over the 2010--2019 period as a result of changes in direct spending and revenue.

![Douglas Elmendorf, CBO Director (2009–2015)](https://drhuyue.site:10002/sammo3182/figure/sci_elemendorf.jfif){fig-align="center" height=100}

:::

::: {.column .fragment width="50%"}

> The health care reform legislation would raise, not lower, federal deficits, by [$562 billion]{.red}.

![Douglas Holtz-Eakin, former CBO Director (2003–2005)](https://drhuyue.site:10002/sammo3182/figure/sci_HoltzEakin.jpg){fig-align="center" height=200}

:::{.fragment}

A gap of [**138 + 562 = \$700 billion**]{.red} 😱

:::

:::{.notes}

Douglas Holtz-Eakin directed the CBO from 2003–2005 and was chief economic policy adviser to Senator John McCain's 2008 presidential campaign — putting him firmly in the Republican camp. His estimate was published by the American Action Forum, a conservative think tank he founded. Both men are credentialed economists who formerly led or directed the same office, yet arrived at opposite conclusions.

:::

:::

::::


## Same data, opposite answers

- Both analyses used [publicly available]{.red} legislative text and economic data
- Key differences in [assumptions]{.red}:
    - Whether certain spending cuts in the bill would actually be implemented
    - How to treat revenues from Medicare provisions
    - Baseline projections for healthcare cost growth
- Neither side fabricated numbers---they made [different modeling choices]{.red}

:::{.notes}

**Holtz-Eakin's critique:** CBO took the bill's stated provisions at face value — even politically unrealistic ones (e.g., sustained Medicare payment cuts). He "corrected" for what he called budget gimmicks.

**Why CBO's convention is still legitimate** (if students push back):

- *Role*: CBO scores policy choices, not political will — if it second-guessed Congress, it would be making policy
- *Discipline*: "What changes if this law passes vs. baseline?" — injecting political predictions contaminates the comparison
- *Anti-gaming*: if CBO discounted provisions, legislators could write aggressive savings they never intend to keep
- *Humility*: predicting which provisions survive future politics is outside CBO's expertise — being wrong destroys credibility

**The real problem with Holtz-Eakin:** his "realism correction" was itself a political judgment (assuming Democrats wouldn't enforce their own bill) dressed as economic analysis.

:::


## The Essence of the problem

> All such estimates are [uncertain]{.red} and that actual future impacts could differ significantly from the estimates of any given organization.

![](https://drhuyue.site:10002/sammo3182/figure/sci_usHHS.webp){fig-align="center" height=400}
